content: Is there anything in the observation that for species the derivative is defined as F′(X)≅F(X+{∙})F'(X) \cong F(X + \{\bullet\})F′(X)≅F(X+{∙})? So here we have F(F(X))≅1+F′(X)F(F(X)) \cong 1 + F'(X)F(F(X))≅1+F′(X).

Just to say it out loud, this says a list of lists of elements in X is either a list containing only the empty list, or a list of elements of X and 'markers': copies of ∙\bullet∙.

But one thing it implies, when taken with other observations here, is that the function

f(x)=11−x \displaystyle{ f(x) = \frac{1}{1-x} }f(x)=1−x1​

obeys the differential equation

f′(x)=f(f(x))−1 f'(x) = f(f(x)) - 1 f′(x)=f(f(x))−1

One thing that's intrigued me for a long time - I've probably discussed it with @David Corfield on the n-Category Cafe - is that

f(f(f(x)))=x f(f(f(x))) = x f(f(f(x)))=x

This always makes me wonder if there's some weak sense in which

X∗∗∗≃X. X^{\ast \ast \ast} \simeq X .X∗∗∗≃X.

I don't think

X∗∗∗≅X, X^{\ast \ast \ast } \cong X , X∗∗∗≅X,

say as endofunctors on Set\text{Set}Set. This would be saying a list of lists of lists of elements of XXX is the same as an element of XXX!

But let's see what we can do with @Vincent Moreau/@Oscar Cunningham's nice fact that

X∗∗≅(X+1)∗+1 X^{\ast \ast} \cong (X + 1)^\ast + 1X∗∗≅(X+1)∗+1

This implies that

X∗∗∗≅((X+1)∗+1)∗, X^{\ast\ast\ast} \cong ((X + 1)^\ast + 1)^\ast, X∗∗∗≅((X+1)∗+1)∗,

where we leave the outer star alone, and also that

X∗∗∗≅(X∗+1)∗+1, X^{\ast\ast\ast} \cong (X^\ast + 1)^\ast + 1 ,X∗∗∗≅(X∗+1)∗+1,

where we leave the inner star alone. Can we do anything with this other than annoying our friends by asking them to show there's a natural isomorphism

((X+1)∗+1)∗≅(X∗+1)∗+1? ((X + 1)^\ast + 1)^\ast \cong (X^\ast + 1)^\ast + 1 \quad ? ((X+1)∗+1)∗≅(X∗+1)∗+1?

John Baez said:

I don't think

X∗∗∗≅X, X^{\ast \ast \ast } \cong X , X∗∗∗≅X,

say as endofunctors on Set\text{Set}Set. This would be saying a list of lists of lists of elements of XXX is the same as an element of XXX!

Indeed, there is no such bijection when XXX is a finite set, because the left hand side is then infinite.

John Baez said:

Can we do anything with this other than annoying our friends

The reason I wanted another formula for (X∗)∗(X^*)^*(X∗)∗ was to represent words of words as simply typed λ\lambdaλ-terms of a certain type (with just ×\times× and ⇒\Rightarrow⇒, no coproducts) -- as described in the second message of the thread -- and to see if this yields an interesting viewpoint on the Kleene star of regular languages. Well, at the end of the day I can still represent non-empty words of words, which is not exactly what I wanted but still better than nothing I guess, and along the way I eventually got interested in the formula itself.

John Baez said:

This implies that

X∗∗∗≅((X+1)∗+1)∗,X^{\ast\ast\ast} \cong ((X + 1)^\ast + 1)^\ast,X∗∗∗≅((X+1)∗+1)∗,

To help intuitions, we might consider Morse code, with X={dot,dash}X = \{dot, dash\}X={dot,dash}. Then X∗X^\astX∗ contains a representative of each letter of the alphabet (and each numeral). X∗∗X^{\ast\ast}X∗∗ contains each word and X∗∗∗X^{\ast\ast\ast}X∗∗∗ contains each sentence.

But to send sentences unambiguously we use spaces. So, (X+1)∗(X + 1)^\ast(X+1)∗ contains all words of a language with the 111, a silence lasting 3 units, marking the boundary of a letter. Then ((X+1)∗+1)∗((X + 1)^\ast + 1)^\ast((X+1)∗+1)∗ contains all sentences with the final 111, a silence lasting 7 units, marking the boundary of a word.
This is indeed a nice example of lists of lists of lists. Is this how the silences are actually used in Morse code?

Yes, that's how they use silences. To end a sentence they seem to use a special combination of dots and dashes, rather than go for an even longer silence.

Marking the trace of X∗∗∗≅XX^{\ast \ast \ast} \cong XX∗∗∗≅X via a new symbol. :grinning:

There is an observation that

(A+B)∗=(A∗B)∗A∗(A + B)^\ast = (A^*B)^\ast A^\ast (A+B)∗=(A∗B)∗A∗

that is, you group all the As to the left of each B together as well as the leftovers at the end.

Anyway, one can then see that from this is follows that

(A+1)∗=(A∗1)∗A∗=(A∗)∗A∗ (A + 1)^\ast = (A^* 1)^\ast A^\ast = (A^*)^* A^* (A+1)∗=(A∗1)∗A∗=(A∗)∗A∗

Then if you add 1 to this, you get

1+(A+1)∗=1+(A∗)∗A∗=(A∗)∗1 + (A+1)^* = 1 + (A^*)^* A^* = (A^*)^*1+(A+1)∗=1+(A∗)∗A∗=(A∗)∗

(the last step uses the 'refolding' iso 1+A∗A=A∗1 + A^* A = A^*1+A∗A=A∗)

This observation that you can group things like this is presumably ancient, but it comes up in the theory of Star height for regular languages (the star height problem is: what is the regular expression for a regular language with the least number of nested *s?)

However, star-height is not based on isomorphisms: as regular languages, $$A^\ast^\ast = A^\ast$$ but these are not isomorphic; the morphism going to the right 'forgets' the internal list boundaries

(one could technically make a bijection here, but that is not connected to the regular-language-equivalence that is used in the theory of star height. Language equivalence is like taking the multivariate polynomial functor from an expression like (A+B)∗A(A+B)^\ast A(A+B)∗A and truncating the index set so there is only one index per (ordered) monomial made up of A, B, etc - aka, a language is just a disjunction of its strings)

As for proving the identity, we can write a Haskell function for the grouping:

group :: [Either a b] -> ([([a], b)], [a])
group xs = go xs ([], [])
  where
    go :: [Either a b] -> ([([a], b)], [a]) -> ([([a], b)], [a])
    go [] r = r
    go (Left a : xs) (lgs, ros) = go xs (lgs, a : ros)
    go (Right b : xs) (lgs, ros) = go xs ((ros, b) : lgs, [])

from which we can extract the proof

(A+B)∗=1+(A+B)∗(A+B)=1+(A∗B)∗A∗(A+B)=1+(A∗B)∗(A∗A+A∗B)=1+(A∗B)∗(A∗B)+(A∗B)∗(A∗A)=(A∗B)∗+(A∗B)∗A∗A=(A∗B)∗(1+A∗A)=(A∗B)∗A∗(A + B)^\ast \\ = 1 +(A + B)^\ast (A + B) \\ = 1 + (A^\ast B)^\ast A^\ast (A + B) \\ = 1 + (A^\ast B)^\ast (A^\ast A + A^\ast B) \\ = 1 + (A^\ast B)^\ast (A^\ast B) + (A^\ast B)^\ast (A^\ast A) \\ = (A^\ast B)^\ast + (A^\ast B)^\ast A^\ast A \\ = (A^\ast B)^\ast (1 + A^\ast A) \\ = (A^\ast B)^\ast A^\ast(A+B)∗=1+(A+B)∗(A+B)=1+(A∗B)∗A∗(A+B)=1+(A∗B)∗(A∗A+A∗B)=1+(A∗B)∗(A∗B)+(A∗B)∗(A∗A)=(A∗B)∗+(A∗B)∗A∗A=(A∗B)∗(1+A∗A)=(A∗B)∗A∗

We use the identity we are proving recursively, but this can be justified in that we are expanding the set and then using the identity on a smaller instance, and the instance size is well-founded.

Still, it's not so easy to find this... (and the proof technique is a bit non-obvious)

Maybe a better proof is that the list is the free monoid, and so we can interpret (A+B)∗(A+B)^\ast(A+B)∗ homomorphically in the monoid over (A∗B)∗A∗(A^\ast B)^\ast A^\ast(A∗B)∗A∗

(Monoid interpretations are just folds, which the above code is an explicit form of)

Anyway, the monoid structure is

unit = ([], [])

(groups1, as) ⋅ ([], as') = (groups1, (as ++⋅ as'))
(groups1, as) ⋅ (((ps, b) : groups2), as') = (groups1 ++ [(as ++ ps, b)] ++ groups 2, as')

If you will forgive the pseudoHaskell.

Anyway, none of this feels very nice but I figured I should send it for completeness

There is a nice proof of this observation in the paper Reason Isomorphically using fixpoint calculus.
